channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Dropout's Newest Trailer for Dimension 20 City Council of Darkness Embraces Some New Kindred
item.content:encoded:
The comedy streaming platform Dropout has released the latest trailer for the newest season of Dimension 20. The actual play's newest season, Dimension 20 City Council of Darkness, continues the series' outrageous comedy performances, but with a new twist: they'll be playing Vampire: The Masquerade.

The Dimension 20 City Council of Darkness Trailer Announcement

In an official press release, Dropout confirmed which Intrepid Heroes would be a part of Dimension 20 City Council of Darkness. The series will premiere on April 8th exclusively on the Dropout platform and consist of 14 episodes. The players are Emily Axford, Ally Beardsley, Lou Wilson, Zac Oyama, Brian Murphy, Siobhan Thompson, and Brennan Lee Mulligan is the show's Game Master.

Or, more accurately, Brennan Lee Mulligan is the show's Storyteller. In a first for the actual play series, Dimension 20 will not be using Dungeons & Dragons, but White Wolf's Vampire: The Masquerade system to fuel its collaborative narrative. This is major since D&D's system has been able to fit the series' many wacky ideas like "Wholesome Mad Max" and "Magic School Hijinx."

It is also a notable departure of tone. Dungeons & Dragons is kitchen sink heroic fantasy, designed for larger than life stories of good and evil. Vampire: The Masquerade is part of White Wolf's World of Darkness setting, a multimedia dark urban gothpunk world of monsters among humanity, centuries-old alliances and conspiracies, and the inner struggles to contain one's savage impulses.

That is, until you see the premise of Dimension 20 City Council of Darkness. As the trailer shows, the players are Kindred who have been exiled by the Camarilla from their ancient castle to the small town of Purpee, Oregon. But, if they can establish vampiric dominion in this town, they can get back in the organization's good graces.

The gag is that Purpee, Oregon is extremely quaint, saccharine, and wholesome, and the group have bumbled their way into running the local government. This means these ruthless creatures of the night will have to manage their blood lust while coordinating bake sales, contend zoning disputes, interface with the neighborhood watch, and hide their true nature as vampires. Bloody shenanigans ensue.

Dimension 20 City Council of Darkness marks not just a new season of the actual play show, but the start of a new direction for World of Darkness.

Last year saw the departure of Jason Carl, who was the brand manager and host of the Vampire: The Masquerade By Night actual play series. In addition the revived White Wolf studio announced a move to internal development of World of Darkness tabletop products, shifting away from contracting the IP to other studios.

Ever since he was small, Tyler Chancey has had a deep, abiding love for video games and a tendency to think and overanalyze everything he enjoyed. This… More about Tyler



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/news/dropouts-newest-trailer-for-dimension-20-city-council-of-darkness-embraces-some-new
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Dark Scrolls Preview - Challenging and Charming
item.content:encoded:
Don't say it, Austin, don't say it...

Dark Scrolls is the Dark Souls of side-scrolling -- actually, you know what, I'm just going to stop there. It's a phrase that's been beaten to death, but the temptation to make a comparison was killing me. Dark Scrolls, the latest title from doinksoft and Devolver, is a challenging experience with a medieval-fantasy aesthetic. It even has a bonfire that heals you! 

But that's about all both games have in common, so let's dive into how this tossed salad of genres that is Dark Scrolls works by going over a preview of its co-op, side-scrolling, shmup, platformer roguelite gameplay. 

Let me preface by saying Dark Scrolls is primarily a co-op game, but it can be played solo. Unfortunately, I'm an adult and all my friends are too, so we barely have any time to see each other anymore. I didn't get a chance to play with any of them, but I'll be damn sure to do so once I get the opportunity. With local or online multiplayer, I believe Dark Scrolls is designed primarily around two players.

That said, even solo, Dark Scrolls provides ample entertainment for those looking for a challenge. At its core, this is a challenging side-scroller invoking feelings of older titles like the original Ninja Gaiden or Castlevania. The screen in Dark Scrolls, however, continuously scrolls, so there is a sense of urgency with every run.

Runs take place across multiple zones, requiring the player to survive each zone on a single life and defeat a boss. Areas have hand-made sections arranged in a random order, true to many roguelites; on top of that, there's a layer of meta-progression, allowing you to purchase upgrades with a currency gained after each run.

Players have a number of characters to choose from, each with different abilities. The skillset of each character themselves are pretty simple. Each one has a different basic attack, jump, and special. Characters do vary greatly in feel, with the gruff, burly Grizz throwing axes in an arc while the wizard Emerys -- wearing her best Vivi Ornitier cosplay -- lobs out magic orbs that bounce around the screen.

The other two characters I tried were Pigeon and Biscuit. The rogueish Pigeon throws a shorter-ranged flurry of knives, while Biscuit -- who is quite literally just a dog -- can bark. Pretty underwhelming, right? Biscuit is pretty cute, and the bark does hurt enemies, to be fair.

Along with standard attacks are jumps and specials, as I mentioned. The jumps all behave differently as well, with Grizz able to ground pound on enemies. You can kind of chain these ground pounds to stay in the air, provided you land on an enemy. Emerys shoots forward and upward like a magic missile, which also damages enemies.

Biscuit's jump is actually the best, though, because he can just continue to jump without the need to hit an enemy. He twirls into a ball like Sonic the Hedgehog and damages enemies, which makes up for Biscuit's admittedly lacking basic bark attack. 

And, as you attack enemies during a run, you'll gain stars that add to your special gauge which, when full, can be unleashed. At the press of a button, Grizz can sneeze out dozens of axes in one big burst. Emerys gets an orb shield that does hurt enemies, but it's more suited for blocking enemy projectiles for a short duration.

Emerys can go into stealth and avoid any and all attacks for a while, so that's also another more defensive-oriented ability that can come in handy in a pinch. Overall, though, I preferred the more offensive style of Grizz, though that may change since there will be a number of other characters in the final release. 

Earn Your Stars

To add another layer to Dark Scrolls' gameplay are upgrades that can modify your character in a number of ways. In each run, you'll gather a lot of coins from enemies, and that's primarily used to buy upgrades from a shop in the middle of a level. These upgrades can do anything from enhance your attack speed or add fire damage to your basic attack, all the way to spawning a fairy that drops hearts to heal you.

You're given only a handful of slots for these upgrades, and these activate once you reach the amount of stars required. As you know, you gain stars as you attack enemies, and those can accrue fast. 

Ideally, you'd want to hold on to your special -- which can only be activated at five stars -- for the right moment, but with each ability being assigned to a specific star, you're incentivized to use that special more often so you can activate these effects. Dark Scrolls didn't explain this mechanic right away, and it might sound convoluted on paper, but it's actually pretty intuitive once you see it in action. 

Maybe I'm washed, but Dark Scrolls was also pretty challenging. With a single life and not all that much health to boot, it truly captures that old-school feel of side-scrolling platformers. Bosses beyond the first level are pretty beefy, and there are a number of hazards in levels on top of enemies you have to deal with. Dark Scrolls is pretty generous in healing you at a bonfire after each level, but that didn't stop me from losing run after run. 

Dark Scrolls Preview | Final Thoughts

With Dark Scrolls' recent reveal, it wasn't the gameplay that captivated me so much as the art style. The spritework is less detailed than the developer's previous title, Gunbrella, but that more retro look complements the simple, old-school gameplay. Just seeing the different monsters, especially the bosses, is a treat. I'm looking forward to seeing more of these designs, provided I can get good and progress far enough. 

Dark Scrolls is a bit like doinksoft's other titles in that you have very simple concepts executed with finesse and charm. Gato Roboto, for instance, was a straightforward Metroidvania involving a cat that can pop in and out of its mech as needed. While Dark Scrolls probably has a lot more to show, especially with its other characters, abilities, and late-game levels, I'm confident that it'll nail the landing and deliver another satisfying experience.

Dark Scrolls was previewed on a PC using a preview build provided by the developers. All screenshots were taken during the preview process.

Austin cut his teeth writing various  fan-fiction stories on the RuneScape forums when he was in elementary school. Later on, he developed a deep love… More about Austin



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/dark-scrolls-preview-challenging-and-charming
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Cairn Sales Climb to 500k Just Two Months After Launch
item.content:encoded:
Developer and publisher The Game Bakers has revealed that its indie climbing sim Cairn has passed another major sales milestone, and given the game's subject matter, that achievement is presumably marked by an actual milestone.

This news was revealed via a post on Steam earlier today, and it's characteristically succinct. The Game Bakers simply thanks players for helping Cairn to pass 500,000 sales and includes a simple piece of artwork showing a route up the game's imposing mountain.

In early February, it was reported that Cairn had managed to rack up 200,000 sales during its launch weekend, so that means the game has more than doubled its sales total in the intervening weeks. I'm sure The Game Bakers will be happy with that result, especially since Cairn is now easily its most-played game on Steam.

If you're not familiar with Cairn, it's the latest game from The Game Bakers, the French studio responsible for boss rush actioner Furi and contemplative couple RPG Haven. It was originally due for release last year, but was delayed for extra polish, as is often the way.

This time around, the developer is tackling the tricky subject of mountain climbing, and the aim with Cairn is to provide a realistic climbing sim that offers intuitive controls and a distinct lack of hand-holding (although not in the climbing itself, because that would end messily).

Story-wise, Cairn revolves around Aava, a climber who's embarking on a mission to scale the never-before-crested summit of Mount Kami. Aava's story intertwines with her efforts to climb the mountain as she meets new people and grapples with what she's left behind.

The Game Bakers promises that each of Cairn's walls "feels like a boss fight", but that you can adjust the game's difficulty if you feel that the mountain is proving just a little too insurmountable.

Cairn is available right now on PC and PlayStation 5.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/cairn-sales-500k
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
